candidates.direction_name: Restore The Best Measured PTX Grouping Window On The Accepted Surface
ranking_notes: Use restore whenever the active branch drifts or the prior variant closed as a loss.
This keeps the loop anchored to the best-known PTX surface before the next replay.
candidates.hypothesis: The latest measured run landed at 24.415696 ms, still +0.251424 ms away from the accepted 24.164272 ms anchor. The fastest way to keep the loop grounded is to replay the exact accepted PTX surface before reopening another branch.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Search drift away from the accepted PTX steady state rather than a missing structural opportunity.
candidates.risk: Low. This is the known accepted PTX anchor surface.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if this replay does not beat the active anchor cleanly or if it broadens beyond an exact bounded historical surface.

candidates.direction_name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit

candidates.expected_bottleneck: A branch-local hot-band scheduling or geometry bottleneck on the dominant kernel path, rather than a pure restore-only action.
candidates.risk: Moderate. This branch stays on the PTX winner surface but its upside is measured in hundredths of a millisecond and the family is easy to over-replay.
